All financial metrics remain solid, and the company maintains investment‑grade ratings, indicating no current risk to the dividend. Moreover, FY2025 free cash flow would have been even stronger — slightly above $600 million — as the reported figure includes $220 million in cash taxes paid related to the transition tax, as the CFO highlighted on the earnings call.

Outlook

While the midpoint of the adjusted EPS outlook came in just $0.01 above the $1.79 market expected, the revenue outlook is meaningfully stronger, as the 6–9% growth range translates into a midpoint of $4.33 billion versus the $4.12 billion consensus. That said, as the market’s reaction also reflects — with the stock declining rather than advancing — the outlook is not directly comparable, given that the company expects to close the Intermex takeover and begin consolidating it starting in Q3 2026.

The CFO provided the following insights regarding the recent Intermex transaction and the impact of higher interest costs:

Our adjusted revenue outlook for 2026 is for 6%-9% revenue growth, inclusive of Intermex, which we now expect to close in the second quarter of this year. Our adjusted EPS for the full year, we believe, will be between $1.75 to $1.85. This includes higher interest expense as we look to refinance our existing notes coming to maturity in Q1, which are currently yielding 1.35% to something closer to today’s interest rate environment.

…and later offered clearer context around the messy revenue guide, noting that organic growth is expected to be flattish — excluding Intermex —which explains the stock’s sell‑off, as it slightly misses the 2026 revenue estimates.

..organic’s going to be closer to flattish, and then Intermex will help us get closer to the 6%-7%. We think we’ll get some positive growth, but call it flattish.

Management expects continued strong growth in consumer services:

We expect consumer services to have another strong year in 2026, as our travel money business is expected to approach $150 million in revenue, up from nearly nothing a few years ago.

Key Growth Initiatives

Within the broader digital strategy, WU management is placing a strong focus on the digital wallet, and the early results are very encouraging:

Our goal is to create a two-sided network that makes it easy for our customers to move funds cross-border while staying within the Western Union ecosystem. In addition to our U.S. Wallet, we are continuing to see strong results out of our wallets in both Argentina and Brazil.

In Brazil now, we have onboarded roughly 20,000 customers since our launch in May of last year, and in the most recent month, we have redirected roughly 5% of all inbound transfers to the country into our wallet. This saves commission expense, as well as gives us an opportunity to increase retention and potentially monetize our receiver base

The slide below illustrates their global wallet initiatives in my view very effectively:

The opportunities within their highly promising stablecoin platform are summarized on the next slide. It is currently in the testing phase, but is expected to go live within the next couple of quarters.

The CEO provided some valuable remarks regarding the latest stablecoin/USDPT dynamics:

...we have successfully minted our first U.S. dollar payment token, USDPT, and have moved it between our Treasury Department and our agents’ wallets.

These pilots are focused on leveraging on-chain settlement to reduce dependency on legacy correspondent banking systems, shorten settlement windows, and improve our capital efficiency. We see significant opportunities for us to move money faster and at lower cost, without compromising compliance or customer trust. These milestones put us on a path to meet our expectation of offering our payment token to the market by the middle of this year. I’m happy to report that we remain on schedule, and we were looking forward to our market launch. Finally, we are expanding our partnerships and capabilities to allow our customers to move and hold stablecoin digital assets and to allow them to have more control in how they manage and move their money. In many parts of the world, being able to hold a U.S. dollar-denominated asset has real value as inflation and currency devaluation rapidly erodes an individual’s purchasing power.

In addition to the exciting digital developments, management also highlighted several significant wins in onboarding large‑scale partnerships on the legacy side of the business. Deutsche Post is an exceptional win, as their paths had diverged just a few years ago, but they ultimately concluded that WU offers the best value in this segment.

Risks

As the CEO highlighted on the earnings call, political risk is currently elevated across the globe and introduces a high degree of uncertainty:

..any given day, an election or a geopolitical change can happen that disrupts the region again for another 3-6 months.

..the landscape for human capital mobility continues to shift each and every day. For example, in the last quarter, we saw an election in Chile that will have implications across the region for immigration and mobility. Also, a change in leadership in Venezuela, for which the implications are still being assessed.

Additional key risks include macro uncertainty, ongoing U.S. immigration policy headwinds, potential regulatory changes, and execution challenges as the company transitions from a legacy remittance model to a more modern fintech platform.

Overall, in my view, the business remains well‑diversified across markets, and its scale and strong financial position provide a meaningful buffer against these risks.

Valuation

In my view, WU remains clearly undervalued across virtually all valuation metrics, and I fully align with the SA A‑grade.

To add further context, I calculated a forward‑looking EV/FCF multiple and arrived at 7.8x. The calculation is based on the midpoint of the company’s estimated $570 million free cash flow outlook provided at the Investor Day in November 2025, and a current Enterprise Value of approximately $4.5 billion (319 million shares at $9, market cap of $2.9 billion, cash of $1.3 billion, and debt of $2.9 billion).

Taking all of this into account, and considering the company’s broader business prospects, I maintain my Buy rating.

Takeaway

While WU’s Q4 FY2025 results and the 2026 outlook were somewhat disappointing — with revenue down 5% year over year and a miss on top‑line expectations — profitability remained ahead of forecasts, and the Intermex‑inclusive guidance still points to growth. Management also highlighted early signs of improvement in the previously challenged North American business six weeks into 2026, offering a degree of encouragement despite the softer headline numbers.

As highlighted on the call, the global political environment remains highly volatile, and elections in certain regions can shift corridor dynamics almost overnight. At the same time, Western Union’s global scale provides meaningful balance: when one region slows, another often accelerates.

Their digital‑first strategy and their role as a "gatekeeper"—introducing legacy remittance customers to digital services, often for the first time—strikes me as an effective way to migrate users into higher‑value digital channels. I also view their stablecoin initiative as very promising. Combined with their new stablecards and digital wallets, the strategy makes strong sense to me, especially in markets facing high inflation — as the majority of developing countries currently do. Overall, I continue to see Western Union as an attractive business going forward.

In my view, the CEO provided an excellent summary during the earnings call of how they view their role in the ever‑evolving global money‑transfer marketplace:

People will continue to move in search of opportunity, education, and family, and Western Union will continue to provide trusted, compliant, and accessible financial services. As the market continues to evolve, we continue to see a shift towards the digital channel, particularly among younger and more technologically savvy customer cohorts.

Given that WU is often labeled as a business that’s supposedly "dying,’" I think it’s fitting to conclude with Mark Twain’s famous words: "The reports of my death are greatly exaggerated."
